Hi all,

I am excited to meet you all. My name is Austin.

For the questions above:

I'm currently deciding what to start working on. I've been meeting up with a philosophy/ linguistic reading group and I've been interested in delving into the math behind linguistics. I recently got a book "Mathematical Methods in Linguistics" that has piqued my interest since it goes into the different algebras of linguistics like Heyting Algebras and linguistic tree lattices. I've also been interested in delving into vulkan/ opengl for the past few months. I've been redoing my undergraduate math courses from my bachelor's degree at self paced doing, doing at least half of all problems from all chapters. I stared this around February 2024 (graduated with my math degree in 2020), and so far it has been pretty interesting to see what professors drop and what sort of things you miss out on by not looking at the other problems.

In my free time I like to play guitar, but I've been in a bit of a slump lately so just delving into new music has been pretty fun. I've been in an "album of the week" group for the past five years and I've really gone deep into finding new music for that group and to feel musically inspired. I've also been making my way through the Fromsoft games recently.

`EDIT`:
As to "what made this community worth joining", I've always wanted to start a group like this but it has gained pretty little traction in the past. I did an honors research project for Phi Theta Kappa when I was attending community college on "Active Learning Communities" and the benefits one gets from participating in one, and I think they are really great purely from feeling motivated to continue learning something. I think learning should be fun, and there is a joy to learning with others. Based on Sylvan's channel, I think it is clear this will be a good group of people to share in learning with.

Hello everyone, 

Pleasure to meet you all. 

I am currently focusing on figuring out my impulsive and obsessive nature when it comes to dopaminergic activities: 
While "locking-in"  for a strech is not an issue, whenever I see results from my positive actions, I often allow myself to over-celebrate, putting me exactly where I was when starting in terms of impulse control and I often realise this only when it is too late, which leads to me living in short phases of high-productivity followed by long ruts.

As for my technical skills, I am doing a lot of reading on typescript and putting that into practice. 
My current approach is: looking at every feature of typescript, understanding it deeply, and going as deep as I need to in the "type-theory" until it becomes clear and intuitive. 

I spend most of my time either working (full-time developer), in the gym or out with my dog. With the occasional session of the roguelike I am into that week, or series I am watching. 

I wanted to join this community because I am excited about the idea of being around (real) people who are motivated to learn and aren't afraid of being labelled as "try-hards". I deeply admire people who work hard, and aspire to be someone who carries that identity comfortably. 
Being around people who I trust to provide criticism or provide their POV when it comes things I am learning is also invaluable to me. 

Looking forward to getting to know the rest of you.

Hi everyone,

Super happy to be here. I'm Alexander. Right now, I'm working on a few projects, including a fully-native Safari ad blocker as well as a CFR-based competitive poker bot. However, I've been thinking more philosophically about my approach to production. Right now the main thing I’m trying to get better at is choosing and executing on fewer projects and general aspirations instead of saying yes to everything that looks impressive on paper or makes me go "wow". At my university it’s very easy to fill my time with problem sets and side projects that don’t actually move me toward any coherent direction, and I’ve noticed that when I’m overcommitted my output is shallow even if I’m working a lot of hours.
​
Practically, that means I’m training two skills: first, being brutally honest on the front‑end about whether a project is interesting to me and compounds my existing strengths (math, systems, and quantitative work), and second, building the discipline to stick with that project long enough to get past the easy wins and into the part where taste and skill actually pays dividends. I care about this because long term I don’t just want to be “another CS/math kid who can code”; I want to be the kind of person whose work is recognizable, both for my own sanity and for the sake of others. This is what I've been spending time on recently: reflecting on who I am and who I wish to become. 

This community is worth joining because it seems to contain exactly the type of people with whom I aspire to spend my time: doing learning for the sake of absorbing wisdom from others who are smarter in a specific domain, while also sharing any wisdom they may have in exchange. I hope to have engaging and philosophical discussions with everyone here, and I look forward to helping this server become as great as it could possibly be.

Hey people,

I am mostly working on my compiler right now. I am designing and implementing a systems programming language that I can use for all of my personal projects.

I have long been a language hopper. I would move from one language to the next picking up various ideas along the way. I don't regret doing this as I actually learned how languages are similar, where they differ and how more broadly: a language is a bundle of trade-offs

There were just too many times where I was writing in a language and wanted some feature (or lack of feature) and I felt dissatisfied. 

At one point I did try contributing a feature to the hare programming language, which I am quite fond of. I didn't follow through with this as I realized I would rather just write mine own language and have zero bureaucracy between feature implementation and me being able to use it.

I digress, if anyone is interested feel free to ask (very WIP project is at https://github.com/leath-dub/iota ). I should have realized language hopping is in the compiler dev learning pipeline 😊. clear.

This has been most of my time outside of work, climbing and meeting friends. But more broadly it is a shift in wanting to focus on longer term projects. This project will likely be years in the making so continued work on it is giving me new strength in tackling longer term projects in a more general sense (e.g. more focused climbing training).

I think it is worth joining this community as I suspect there will be some geeky or philosophical debate that I want a word in on. I really like constructing models of the world by thinking and discussing with my peers so I look forward to the same here!

Hey everyone,

I'm currently learning about the theory behind compilers and how to implement them. I'm almost done with crafting interpreters and after that I want to spend some time working on a small language. I eventually want to experiment with C and see if it can be made safer through tooling or by adding things to its runtime (eg. bounds checking). In the past I've messed around with OpenGL a lot. I've built some engines and tech demos in Zig and C++. 

Outside of tech I enjoy doing photography and playing table tennis. I've recently been trying to read books as well.

I wanted to join this community because I'd like to be around people who know more than me and from whom I can learn from. I enjoy talking to people about technical stuff so I hope this community can foster an environment like that.

Hi everyone,

I'm a maths student currently doing a Master's, which takes up most of my time. My main area of interest is in algebraic geometry, which at the stage I'm at is mostly absorbing as much formalism as possible before you can get to actually doing things. I have a lot of subjects to assimilate, and the vague understanding of what I've learned so far is something I need to make clear in my head. My experience in compsci stuff is pretty minimal beyond a little python as a teenager, but I ended up using emacs (with evil obviously) somehow.

Recently in my free time I've been getting into philosophy and I started reading Metaphysics by Aristotle. As a work of philosophy, it's highly influential and by understanding it you unlock the ability to understand the other writings about metaphysics/ontology that later philosophers have written. Mathematics is essentially reasoning about things independently of sense-experience, so metaphysics seems like a natural pairing. I'm not sure how much I buy into metaphysics as a project, or at least what Aristotle has to say about it, but it's important enough to justify reading about. Plus, even if the book is hard, its easier than math textbooks.

This community seems consist of people who have the impulse to optimise as much as possible, and to do things efficiently (and properly). While there are plenty of people who are like this in isolation, there really isn't a space online with people who care about things that matter, and put effort into these things on a meta sort of level (ie obsessing with workflow, how to do things well, finding the best ways to think and learn, etc.).  There's an obvious focus on compsci stuff here, but the interests of people seem pretty general (philosophy, Buddhism, psychology). The former is a weak spot in my knowledge which would be good to build on, and the general interests of the community mean there's a lot of things I'd get to know about that I wouldn't otherwise encounter.

Hello everyone,
My name is Max and I am currently a Master's student in a mathematical biology lab in Tokyo, but most of my background is in cognitive psychology/neuroscience. In my research I am currently looking at methods to detect mental fatigue by measuring multiple physiological signals at the same time (brain, heart, breathing, eye movement) as a subject goes through various mentally demanding tasks. Through this we hope to make it clear when someone should stop working or switch task loads to optimize a healthy balance of work and rest, based on their individual physiology. If you want to ask more about this project please feel free to reach out! 

In my free time I have really enjoyed spending time on academic writing. At first I started it as a way to practice for when I submit research, but I ended up really falling in love with the idea of writing down something of importance for an audience. People often say that they have their own specific writing style, but I have recently been very cerebral as to who I am writing to (e.g. writing a paper for medical professionals vs for engineers). This talk by Larry McEnerney really inspired me to think about value in this way and I highly recommend it https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vtIzMaLkCaM

I am currently I also have been lightly studying philosophy that relates to scientific research. I have been enjoying Dr. Michael Levin's lectures on platonic forms and bioelectricity, as they bridge a lot of the neuroscience, genetics, and even AI research that I read in my day-to-day in my lab with ancient hypothetical understandings about the universe.

I felt this community was worth joining because of the pure nature of its goal in the search of knowledge. I am the type of person who likes to bounce ideas off of others and having a judgement-free community of self aware peers has the potential to be an incredible well of knowledge and idea reflection. I am really excited for the chance to talk with you all!

Hey everyone! I'm Dean, a student at Stanford currently finishing up my undergrad in math/physics and in the middle of my master's in computer science. I'm particularly interested in differential geometry and algebraic topology. I also love to teach, and hope to pursue a PhD in mathematics in the future to one day become a professor.

On top of school I'm also working part-time at Math, Inc (https://math.inc), a new startup building AI agents for mathematical autoformalization. We're partnering with leading mathematicians to accelerate the process of verifying research-level mathematics and working towards superintelligence. Essentially, we're building machinery to automate theorem proving in Lean 4. (formalization is an incredibly exciting field of mathematics/computer science right now; would love to talk to you all about it!! it's gotten a lot of attention on twitter recently if any of you have seen that lol)

Outside of academics I'm also really into music; I play the piano (classical + jazz)/drums/guitar, produce, audio engineer, and DJ. I am a huge music nerd and would welcome any music recommendations y'all have!

I've been watching Sylvan's videos for a while now and really admire the audience that has amassed around the channel. It's clear that all of you who have joined/will join are super driven and capable people, and being part of a community of those types can be incredibly powerful for personal growth. I also just love talking to nerdy people about nerdy things

super excited to meet you all! :)

Hi, everyone!

I'm a computer science undergraduate doing research in *interpretable deep learning* and *evolutionary optimization*. I've been investigating whether evolutionary and gradient-based optimizers produce neural networks with distinct learning patterns and internal representations. I aim to pursue a Ph.D. in Japan upon graduation, and am looking forward to establishing a life and research career there.

I've been trying to improve my ability to read technically dense material and think about problems mathematically—in their purest form. In this spirit, I've transitioned from Python to Julia, which has allowed me to write optimization algorithms from scratch using native linear algebra operators and Unicode symbols, while achieving C-like performance. Through this, I'm hoping to gain a clear, profound understanding and sense of control over the optimization process. I've also recently moved to a NixOS and Emacs setup (inspired by *LCOLONQ*), which has been incredibly fun to use and tinker with!

I strongly resonate with the ideas Sylvan discusses in his channel, which I find quite hard to come by amidst the jungle of noise on the internet. I've also been particularly invested in fostering my intrinsic motivation, following my interests, and distancing myself from the LinkedIn carrot-driven world. This community seems to be a refuge for people who feel similarly, and I'm excited to see what stems from it.

Pleasure meeting you all!

Hello all, right now I'm learning Nix & NixOS, because I'm trying to run services on local machines & VPSs and I want configurations to be fully reproducible. There's nothing worse than trying to fix an issue on a Debian VPS that's been working fine for months, only to connect to it and to have forgotten how everything was set up. So, I hope learning Nix will help with this. I'm also trying to get a more comprehensive understanding of networking, and I'm also dabbling into statistics. It's been a grind because I'm doing this all outside of a university setting, but I enjoy the rigor.

I got into Rationalism about four months ago, and since then I've been enjoying challenging my own biases & improving my thought processes. It seems like a lot of things on LessWrong are just re-inventing existing philosophical concepts in a more technical context, but the vibrancy of the community has been alluring, and everything has been relatively easy to read despite the jargon, so that's what keeps me on it.

I love surrounding myself with more intelligent & knowledgeable people than me, and I'm a bit of an autodidact. Because of these two things, I try to find real-life and online community where I can get more intelligent & knowledgeable through both osmosis, and hard work on my end. So I hope to ask good questions and stay humble in sight of all the awesome people here :)

Hi everyone, I am Ishuman and I am a compsci sophomore.

Right now, I am on a pause from coding because of uni exams. Before this, I was trying to learn the web stack from the fundamentals where I kept running into the dilemma of deep understanding vs just implementing. I was spending way too long on things that should have been quick - like wrestling with the urge to read the entire MDN documentation before writing code, trying to find the balance between ‘deep’ comprehension and speed. So, I’m using this break to reset by reading books (like Ultralearning) to build a clear mental framework for myself when I get back to working on my projects again; but I am aware of the fact that reading about productivity may be a more polished form of procrastination, so I am treating this as a gamble I am willing to take.

I’ve been spending some time lately thinking about how most of my ideas are often 'diet' versions of what they could’ve been - interesting, but underdeveloped (un-articulated) ideas. I want to find a way to manage the noise of having too many open loops in my head. The answer may just be aggressive prioritization, but I will have to experiment with this a bit more.
After a recent conversation with my mum, I have also been trying to let go of the idea of an imminent 'stable state' and accept that my idea of stability is largely a delusion. This is something I am still trying to come to terms with.

Apart from that (& finals), I am trying to spend more time being present with my family and listening to music.

This community felt worth joining because I was genuinely taken aback by how closely the questions asked here mirror the ones I’ve been tackling in isolation. It is also very inspiring to see how obsessed everyone here is with their craft. I am here to absorb as much as possible and expose myself to new perspectives alongside peers who are working through similar challenges.

I am very excited to meet everyone here :)

Hello,

I'm an Engineer & SysAdmin (working in Broadcast Tech) as well as an aspiring Game Dev working on a few Projects.
I got into programming as a hobby as a kid back when Newgrounds made Adobe Flash the premier medium, forcing java down our throats.
During that period, I got into no-code tools for making basic Games like RPG Maker 2000 and eventually fell off once I went to college.
Since then, I picked programming back up as a hobby and eventually used it to push myself further in my career, moving from production into this new role.
Now I'm working on getting back to my roots, working on games and video processing tools in my free time.

Current Projects:
1. Creating a Roguelike Turn Based RPG in the style of Pokémon/SMT with a large focus on side effects and composibility 
2. Building an 'autologger' program which automatically downloads and captions videos and enters this info into a database to be fuzzy-searched.
The goal is to create a better tool for content prep for content creators and newsrooms
3. I work as an Engineer at a Broadcast Hub where I create a variety of programs for all manner of tasks related to basic SysAdmin tasks, as well as some projects with ffmpeg or polars for automating workflows, etx

Lately, I've been diving into the 'dark side' of programming- front end- in order to create GUI interfaces for users at work, as well as my future projects.

I'm joining in order to build connections with the other side of the programming world.
As a Terminal Andy with no web development experience, I'm looking for a clear exchange of info with that other half of the software world

Hi, I am a software engineering student, who is trying to understand the world from newer and newer perspectives, integrating them into a whole. Nowadays in my free time I mainly watch video essays and read books on psychology, philosophhy, and esotericism. Lately what I enjoy the most is trying to put myself in the shoes of people living before our world built on science and clear rationality, trying to understand what essence they tried to communicate with their more abstract philosophical/esoteric works, and how (if at all) they can be translated into our current experience. I joined this community because many of Sylvan's thoughts and interests resonate with mine, and I hope that I can thus find many other likeminded people here with interesting perspectives.

Hello, I'm currently pursuing a Bachelor's in Human Biology and in my down time I do research on Bioenergetics and Cell Biology. I've currently been thinking about Bioelectricity and interdisciplinary fields in general. More specifically how combining two seemingly unrelated fields of study can open up new frontiers in knowledge and make clear certain fields previously considered nebulous. Nowadays I enjoy reading books about cybernetics and post-modern philosophy, I also like to browse rationalist forums like LessWrong. I thought this community was worth joining because I would be able to learn from people with a common zeal. I also believe that people from different fields can learn a lot from each other and contribute to fields other than their own, and I think this community is a good way to achieve that.

Hi everyone my name is Kenji, I’m a final-year Software Engineering student currently diving deep into the Android Framework (Core/HAL) for my internship.
Right now, I’m navigating the tension between productivity and curiosity. I’ve shifted my focus from standard app development to the low-level "plumbing" of systems because I have this persistent itch to understand exactly how software talks to hardware. This transition has been humbling I often find myself stuck in rabbit holes, wrestling with Linux Kernel concepts or obsessively refactoring my workflow instead of actually shipping code. I’m trying to accept that this "tinkering" is actually how I learn best, even if it feels slow or inefficient sometimes.
Lately, I’ve also been thinking about the balance between mastering the fundamentals and exploring the unknown. While I’m grinding LeetCode to solidify my algorithms for the long run, I’ve surprisingly found myself drawn to Quantum Computing, a topic completely out of my depth, but one I’m starting to explore just for the joy of being a beginner again.
This community felt worth joining because I’m looking for an clear escape from the "noise" of typical tech spaces. I miss genuine, deep technical exchanges that aren't driven by hype. I’m here to learn from how you all approach your craft, share my own messy notes on system architecture, and connect with people who are also trying to figure things out.

Hello yall my name is Emilien. I'm a high school senior still trying to figure out what to study later. Been coding since I was about 9 y/o. Fav language is rust, most used is typescript w/ svelte. Also took Latin for four years and am just starting out learning philosophy. 

I'm currently applying to college and working on a few projects. I'm getting back into reading and thinking more after I read Amusing Ourselves to Death. 

I'm drawn to this community in the hopes that it will be the first I will actually like to take part in. I am part of maybe 20 discord servers, and check none on the daily. This one seems like it really could be different, because it's trying to be a more curated/genuine internet experience. I wasn't around for the early internet, but I think this is more what it would've looked like. I'm also drawn to it because I feel like I'll find engineers who enjoy the holistic process more here. More and more I feel like some really "smart" people (according to society's clear definition) are basically braindead but can solve complex physics problems easily.

I'm trying to become technically excellent but with a good understanding and appreciation of the humanities, and this seems like the perfect place for it. Looking forward to chatting with yall.

Hey everyone, my name is Pascal. I am currently in college and trying to broaden my understanding in as many disciplines as I can with keen interested in web development, philosophy, maths, Operating systems, networking and cybersecurity.

I am particularly interested in this community because I resonate the many of the topics Sylvan Franklin addresses in his videos ans other interactions. It is also clear to me that the interactions of several members in the community have with each other within but not exclusive to the github issues discussions are those which I prefer.
I aim here is to getting familiar with more people whom I can hold deep and insightful conversations with. I would also like to use this platform as a way I can share my experiences, brain teasers, and projects im working on.

I look forward to talking you everyone.

Hello everyone. I'm Yunus, a American/Turkish highschool senior who's been programming in various fields ranging from graphics programming to backend development. I got my start in programming making Minecraft Forge mods in Java.

Currently I'm working on a few side projects as well as my main lifelong commitment. My side projects are as follows:
**yt-dlp-operator**: A k8s operator for automating yt-dlp download jobs. Nothing fancy but it helped me learn more about Golang and test-driven development.
**SVN hosting platform**: A web platform for hosting Apache Subversion repos, kind of like Github but on a smaller scale, with manual repo creation by me and an approval process(for now). SVN is the clear winner for version control for game development because of how well it handles large binaries, which is why I chose it.
**psyslop-desktop**: A fake web OS frontend for thavlik's "Psyslop" project, sort of part of an ARG. It uses a database instead of a filesystem, inspired by WinFS and Project 9. This was initially a very unserious project but its helped me visualize what my lifelong project could look like.
**PX-CORE**: A monolithic game engine being worked on as my final project for my Comp Sci class. React to this message with a white heart emoji. Written with minimal dependencies in C++, with Win32 API and DirectX 11.

My lifelong commitment is to create a unified software and hardware ecosystem for people who value productivity. Specifically, my initial target audience is power users, office users and enterprise. This project is under my company, Trioch (to be registered). The ecosystem as it stands currently can be broken up into a few pieces:
**1. Atlas**: A stable, secure operating system with a microkernel architecture. There are many interlaced systems of Atlas, so I'll just briefly go over some of them here. Firstly, the Lifetime Manager ensures that each isolated component of the OS runs as expected, and performs atomic A/B updates on each component.
1/2

Hiya! I'm a GameDev, previously worked in Robotics.

Currently I'm working on a suite of "serious" libraries for my "game" engine: 
- [meta](https://github.com/UsatiyNyan/serious-meta-library) - template magic
- [exec](https://github.com/UsatiyNyan/serious-execution-library) - asynchronous execution, channels/futures and such
- [gfx](https://github.com/UsatiyNyan/serious-graphics-library) - OpenGL wrapper
- [game](https://github.com/UsatiyNyan/serious-game-library) - experiments in combining ECS with robust asset management and asynchrony
Technologies of choice are C/C++ and CMake, because they are fine and also because I've already started with them and it's too late to move to Rust (but I will consider Zig in the future).
End goal is to create data visualization for robotics ecosystem and at least a "game", for now.

Lately I've been very derailed into math and resurrecting my old [music visualizer](https://github.com/UsatiyNyan/serious-music-visualizer) project.
In terms of math - I'm trying to rebuild my intuition and try to solve correctness in my "exec" library.
I'm very much enjoying Math Academy rn.

I wanted to join, since I'm in search of a community of as passionate about computer stuff people as I am. And I genuinely like teaching and mentoring (so if you have any questions about aforementioned technologies - feel free to ask).
I acknowledge, that I’m coming in with a baggage of past experience, but willing to learn from others and listen — I have several hard and interesting questions, which I hope we will be able to discuss together.

EDIT: project description corrections, and corrections to 3rd section, and also “clear”

Hello, everybody! My name is Leon, and I'm a CS major (in my final semester) from Salzburg, Austria.
My main interests are ML and cybersecurity, so that's where I focus most of my energy, but I have recently taken an interest in systems after doing a C++ internship.
Since I'm about to graduate, most of my time is currently spent working on my thesis, which is about the temporal robustness of CNN-based malware detection. It's been **clear** from the beginning that this would be a challenge but it has been very worthwhile and I am learning a ton.

I feel like everyone else has listed some of their work, so here it goes:
- **Research Project:** Dynamic target value optimization in artificial neural networks
- **Side project:** Discord Bot for easy file sharing between my phone and PC (I know it's a privacy nightmare; don't @ me).
- **Side project:** Static site generator for Obsidian-style Markdown files
- **Uni project:** Synthetic image detection (vascular biometrics)

I'm honestly not too proud of these projects, but I hope to learn a lot from others in here!

In 2026 I plan to:
- Get actually good at SQL, no more just doing SELECT * FROM ... WHERE ...
- Learn Docker
- Build a start-to-finish ML pipeline and deploy it (the model can be bad! I just want to get the full stack under my belt).
- Start my Masters

Maybe some of you here are cracked at this stuff already and have some things to teach me! :)

(PS: I also make music but that is on hold until I'm done with my thesis)

Hi all,

I’m Ian, a 1st year PhD student in nuclear engineering. I primarily focus on robotics and am starting work on a project using multicopters for radiation mapping. I love robot math. State estimation and nonlinear control systems are what get me up in the morning. In undergrad, one of my favorite projects was an aquatic robot named “Gurgle,” a tethered ROV. 

Over the last few years I’ve gotten interested in computer graphics. I was dissatisfied by the lack of programming infrastructure for Blender and so I am headed to source (I tried to program movement protocols for objects but there was not a clear path in Blender). I would like to finish learning OpenGL so that I can make my drone control system simulator and make cool visuals for my research. Making dynamic visuals is the most fun way to do research imo. 

I found vimothy through his typst tips and have now evangelized. I recently completed a hefty software project during an internship (a pipeline for parallelizing HPC jobs). Unfortunately, I no longer have the mentorship afforded by the software engineers I worked with. I got addicted to the process and want to continue maturing as a developer. That’s what made the idea of this community seem valuable. I love to learn from people who are devoted to what they do and indeed if you have nuclear science questions, just ask. The people here seem to share a voracious appetite for robust development practices and clever uses of software tools and I look forward to seeing what is made of this community.

Hi, I'm a 22 year old recent grad currently upskilling myself so that I may land my dream software engineering job. Pleased to be your guys acquaintance. 

I have been thinking about how to best teach CS to young people. Over the past year, I tutored college students as a side hustle. My love for programming stems from its accessibility, you only need a twelve hour YouTube video to get your foot in this field. There is no obscene amount of gatekeeping. I believe developing is easy if taught in the proper way. Students at my school were complaining about the curriculum being unnecessarily contrived, so my work as an educator has been focused on fixing this problem. Professor David Malan from Harvard University is one of my idols. I aspire to educate people around the globe like him someday. 

[This](https://discord.com/oauth2/authorize?client_id=1455036822014001168&permissions=68608&integration_type=0&scope=bot) is a Discord Bot I made for another server that lets you query information on Pokemon. It is not as impressive as what I see on other people's resumes, but I love 'marnie bot' because it's something I made from scratch myself. I've dealt with imposter syndrome throughout my entire four years of college. My peers engineered useful software while I sat around playing Overwatch. Marnie Bot represents the fact that I have the capability to do stuff if I try. 

I joined this community out of an enjoyment for Vimothy's content and a desire to meet developers better than me. I am aware of a _clear_ skill gap between me and other new grads. Personally, simply being in the presence of strong thinkers inspires me to do better. I hope to meaningfully contribute to conversations here instead of doomscrolling Twitter. When I scroll the Twitter 'for you' page, I feel an overwhelming sense of dread. I have since deleted my account there entirely. 

I want to become a better person in 2026 by spending my time in places like this.

Hey guys, my name is Emilio (or EloMilo, whichever you prefer).
I'm a 21 year old nursing student in Austin, Texas, aspiring to be a physician (or any medical provider) one day.
I work in the medical field as a medical assistant (fun fact: I didn't need to be a doctor to put the defibrillator pads on a patient and say, "Clear!").

I'm excited to be a part of this community. In my college, I see little aspiration from peers. I used to go into my lectures roleplaying as if I pulled up to the School of Athens.
Currently, some of my technical interests include understanding computer science (I love learning about modern hardware such as CPU architectures, photolithography, machine learning/AI, computer languages, cybersecurity, homelabbing, etc.), all maths, physics, chemistry, and biology. EVERYTHING medical (neuroscience, cardiology, pharmacology, psychiatry/psychology - especially metacognition), astronomy & astrophysics, and philosophy. Anthropology (religions, civilization, etc.) business and finance (quantitative finance personally seems really obfuscated for some reason?), blue-collar professions (automotive technology, carpentry, HVAC, electrical, welding), music (my two instruments are a guitar and MIDI keyboard).
Some experiential interests are PC & VR gaming, watching films, shows, sports, comedy, anime, YouTube video essays, broadening my music knowledge, going to parties/bars/clubs, cooking, aphorisms and poems, and weightlifting/fitness.
I'm always told how much my friends and family think I should become a professor for a medical school rather than work in hospitals, so some of y'all may benefit from my explanations.

I joined this community after a week of binge-watching Sylvan's content (the dry humor in his YouTube shorts is next level). I'm glad I found people so like-minded. I have, for the past year, been working on developing a game and engine effectively from scratch in Java with no dependencies other than LWJGL's OpenGL bindings. The basic premise for the world itself, is that the player should not be limited at all in where they can travel, and I mean that to the maximal degree; it would take longer than the heat death of the universe to reach the edge of the map. If a star is visible, it can be reached, and will have planets with moons fully fleshed out with content orbiting it. Admittedly, this setting sounds a lot like No Man's Sky (and every space game ever), however I believe the new thing I have to offer to players in terms of gameplay with this project is this philosophy of maximal freedom. Everything within reason is fully simulated, such that even the individual lines of circuitry in the computer of the player's ship are being fully simulated in world, and can be modified at will at any time by players. Think Minecraft redstone circuitry if it ran in real time, or Noita if it was with voxels and with much more mechanical depth than simple alchemical reactions. Currently, I am working on separating this project into a client and server, as I realised how cool it would be to be able to play this with multiple people in one universe. Knowing how the things that I'm using work under the hood is highly important to me.

My contemporary hobbies include, but are not limited to, in no particular order: playing the piano, playing the guitar, playing Stephen's Sausage Roll at the recommendation of Jonathan Blow, etymology, health and nutrition, and data hoarding.

So right now I'm working on the release of my project's v1. Probably this week! I'm building a browser extension named Steer to enforce intentional work using a four-phase workflow: Dump -> Shape -> Commit -> Focus. 

1. Dump captures free-form thoughts. 
2. Shape converts them into a single enforceable daily outcome using rules: single-outcome focus, energy-weighted task priority, and constraint-first ordering, with time estimates. 
3. Commit locks the plan into a time-boxed daily timeline synced with external calendars. 
4. Focus enforces the committed plan by blocking or allowing browsing in real time according to goals.

For v1, only Focus is made. Basically a context-based distraction blocker. Say you wanna "Learn Flutter", you will only be allowed to navigate to pages which are related to that goal.
